key_insight: An interpretable urine-based machine-learning score (uLCI) combining four metabolites and clinical factors detected lung cancer well in development and remained effective in independent validation.
clinical_relevance: If further confirmed, uLCI could provide a non-invasive way to support earlier lung cancer detection beyond smoking-based LDCT eligibility.
method: Lasso-regularized logistic regression integrated four urinary metabolites plus age, race, and smoking.
Model was developed with 10-fold cross-validation in the NCI-Maryland cohort.
It was then applied without refitting to an independent Colorado cohort.
evidence_signal: uLCI achieved AUC 0.906 in the development cohort and 0.748 in independent validation, with increasing scores across cancer stages and prognostic separation after resection.
watch_item: Validation performance dropped versus the development cohort, so prospective testing and clinical workflow validation are still needed before use.
paper_title: The urinary-metabolite-based lung cancer index (uLCI): an interpretable machine-learning risk model for early-stage disease
